title: Framed Three-Shelf Bookshelf
summary: A three-shelf bookcase wrapped in 1x2/1x3 face frames that hide the plywood edges and add built-up legs. Build it from 1x12 pine or ripped plywood strips.
description:
This is a versatile three-shelf bookcase designed to look built-in. A plain shelf box gets wrapped front and back in 1x2 and 1x3 face frames, which does three things at once: it hides the raw edges if you built from plywood, it stiffens the case, and — where the leg pieces run long past the box — it creates the built-up 'legs' that lift the whole thing off the floor by 3-1/2".

The plan is deliberately flexible on materials. Build the box from solid 1x12 pine, or rip a sheet of 3/4" plywood into four 11-1/4" strips and use those instead. Same for the top: a planked pine top (1x3 plus 1x12) or a single plywood/MDF panel, your choice. If you go plywood, you'll iron on edge banding to finish the raw edges — cheap and easy, about $6 for a 25-foot roll.

Assembly order matters for the legs. Attach the side leg pieces (1x2) first, keeping outside edges flush; they over-extend the sides by 3-1/2". Then the front and back legs (1x3) fasten to the side legs, the shelves, and the sides all at once — that overlap is what makes the legs solid rather than tacked-on trim. Trim out each shelf edge, panel the back, cap the top, fill, sand, and finish. Furring-strip pine keeps it cheap; poplar or maple if you're painting fine.

tools.note: Speeds the trim; a hammer works.
Only if you build from plywood and want finished edges.

materials.name: 1x12, 8 ft
materials.note: Sides and shelves — or one sheet of 3/4" plywood ripped into 11-1/4" strips.

materials.name: 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Furring strips work; leg sides, trim.

cutList.note: Measure and cut for best fit.
Planked pine (1x12 + 1x3) or a single plywood/MDF panel.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body: Nail through the sides into the shelves to build the basic box, following the shelf spacing in the plan. Screws or 3/4" pocket holes with 1-1/4" pocket screws also work. Keep it square — the face frames will reference these edges.

steps.title: Attach the side legs
steps.body: Nail the 1x2 leg sides to the sides of the bookcase with 1-1/4" nails, keeping outside edges flush. They over-extend the bottom of the sides by 3-1/2" — that overhang becomes the leg. Confirm both sides extend the same amount so the case sits level.

steps.title: Attach the front and back legs
steps.body: Fasten the 1x3 leg fronts and backs to the side legs with 2" nails, driving into the side legs, the shelves, and the sides all at once. Fastening into all three layers is what makes the legs structural rather than trim stuck on the outside.

steps.title: Trim the top and shelves
steps.body: Attach the inside top trim with 1-1/4" brads and glue, then the two outer top trim pieces, top edges flush. Trim the front edges of all three shelves with the remaining 1x2 pieces, top edges flush with the shelves, using 2" nails and glue. This hides the shelf edges and gives a consistent front face.

steps.title: Panel the back and add side trim
steps.body: Attach the 1/4" back panel with 1-1/4" nails and glue, marking and nailing into every shelf so it can't rattle. Then add the side shelf-trim pieces — measure and cut each for best fit, since real-world spacing drifts from the drawing.

steps.title: Cap the top and finish
steps.body:
Attach the top. For a plywood/MDF top, position it with a 1" overhang on the front and sides, glue and nail, then iron on edge banding to hide the raw edges. For a planked pine top, attach the 1x3 first (back edge flush with the back of the 1x2), 1" overhang each side, then the 1x12 across the front the same way.

Fill nail holes, sand, apply edge banding if you used plywood, and stain or paint.

images.alt: Three-shelf bookshelf with face-frame trim and built-up legs
